concept_name: A Current Loop Acts as a Magnetic Dipole
source_book: DC Pandey Class 12 Ch.4 Moving Charges and Magnetism §4.10 (Magnetic dipole moment of a revolving electron / current loop) — table-of-contents reference only; teaching authored from first principles. NCERT consulted for the Indian real-world anchor only. The atomic concept taught here is the FIELD EQUIVALENCE: a current-carrying loop SOURCES a magnetic field whose external pattern is identical to a bar magnet's dipole field, so the loop IS a magnetic dipole with moment m = N I A (a vector along the loop axis by the right-hand rule). One face acts as a north pole, the other as south — never a single pole. Placed in an external field it feels a torque and aligns like a compass, exactly as a bar magnet does. This is distinct from torque_on_current_loop_in_field, which teaches the τ = μ × B rotational dynamics on a rectangular loop; here the centre of gravity is the field the loop produces and its identity with a bar magnet. The deepest student confusions targeted: (a) a loop and a bar magnet are fundamentally different things, (b) the moment is just a number not a vector, (c) the loop has a single N pole, (d) magnetism needs a permanent magnet / magnetic monopoles exist. Conceptual-only ship (no mode_overrides). EPIC-L-first (no epic_c_branches; misconceptions confronted inside EPIC-L via Rule 16a misconception_watch + predict→reveal).

real_world_anchor.primary: Picture the moving-coil galvanometer or ammeter in your school physics lab — the brass-cased meter whose needle swings across a paper scale when current flows. Inside is a small coil suspended in the field of permanent magnets. That coil is a current loop; it behaves exactly like a tiny bar magnet, so the external field grabs it and turns it. The pointer deflection you read is a current loop acting as a magnetic dipole.
real_world_anchor.secondary: Picture the coil spinning inside a ceiling-fan or mixer-grinder motor on a Bengaluru summer afternoon. Each turn of the winding is a current loop sitting in the stator magnets' field. Because every loop acts as a magnetic dipole, the field pushes them around — that continuous turning is what drives the blades.
real_world_anchor.tertiary: Picture a single electron orbiting the nucleus inside an atom. That orbit is nature's smallest current loop, so it is a tiny magnetic dipole with its own m = N I A. Add up countless such loops in a piece of iron and you get a bar magnet — this is where all magnetism ultimately comes from.

N.name: Number of turns in the loop

tau_magnitude.formula: N * I * (PI * radius * radius) * B * sin(theta_deg * PI / 180)

formulas.m_definition: m = N I A — the magnetic dipole moment of the loop. A vector along the loop axis; direction by RHR (curl fingers along the current, thumb gives m).
formulas.area_circle: A = π r² — the area of the circular loop.
formulas.field_equivalence: Far from the loop, the magnetic field pattern is identical to a bar magnet of the same dipole moment m. The current loop IS a magnetic dipole.
formulas.two_poles: The face the field lines leave is a north pole; the face they enter is a south pole. A dipole always has two poles — there is no magnetic monopole.
formulas.tau_in_field: τ = m × B — in an external field the loop feels a torque that turns it until m aligns with B (stable at θ = 0°).
formulas.origin_of_magnetism: Electrons orbiting and spinning in atoms are tiny current loops; their dipole moments are the microscopic origin of all permanent magnetism.

constraints: The field-equivalence to a bar magnet holds for the EXTERNAL field (far from the loop); very close to the wire the two differ.
m = N I A holds for ANY planar loop shape (circle, square, triangle); the circle is the easy case, not a special one.
B is treated as uniform across the loop in the torque/alignment states.
The loop is rigid and the wire non-deformable; geometry is preserved as it turns.
There is no magnetic monopole — the loop's two poles (N and S faces) are inseparable.
No induced EMF / back-reaction is included here — electromagnetic induction belongs to the next chapter.

aha_moment.statement: A current loop's external field is identical to a bar magnet's.
aha_moment.visual_confirmation: STATE_4 places a bar magnet beside the loop; both field patterns are drawn by the same generator, so the loops of field lines coincide in shape — the loop IS a dipole.

scene_composition.text: A circular loop carrying a steady current I

Yellow ring = current-carrying loop
Orange dots = current flowing round it
Watch the dots — that is I going around.
Same coil as the lab galvanometer — a loop of wire with current in it.
Question: does this plain loop of current do anything magnetic?

tts_sentences.text_en: Here is a circular loop of wire carrying a steady current.
tts_sentences.text_hi: यहाँ देखो — एक circular loop of wire जो steady current carry कर रहा है।
tts_sentences.text_te: ఇక్కడ చూడండి — ఒక circular loop of wire, అది steady current carry చేస్తోంది.

tts_sentences.text_en: Watch the orange dots. They flow around the ring — that is the current, going round and round.
tts_sentences.text_hi: Orange dots को ध्यान से देखो। वो ring के चारों तरफ flow कर रहे हैं — यही है current, जो round and round जा रही है।
tts_sentences.text_te: Orange dots చూడండి. అవి ring చుట్టూ flow అవుతున్నాయి — అదే current, round and round వెళ్తోంది.

tts_sentences.text_en: It looks ordinary. The question is simple. Does this loop do anything magnetic?
tts_sentences.text_hi: यह तो सामान्य लगता है। Question simple है। क्या यह loop कुछ magnetic करता है?
tts_sentences.text_te: ఇది సాధారణంగా కనిపిస్తుంది. Question చాలా simple. ఈ loop ఏమైనా magnetic గా చేస్తుందా?

scene_composition.text: A current loop SOURCES a magnetic field

Predict before the reveal:
what shape do the field lines make
around a loop of current?

Lines leave one face,
curl around through space,
and return to the other face —
closed loops, not a flat circle.
Moving charge makes a magnetic field — so a loop of current does too.

tts_sentences.text_en: Moving charge always makes a magnetic field. The current in this loop is moving charge, so it must make a field.
tts_sentences.text_hi: Moving charge हमेशा एक magnetic field बनाती है। इस loop में current moving charge है, तो यह field जरूर बनाएगी।
tts_sentences.text_te: Moving charge ఎప్పుడూ ఒక magnetic field తయారు చేస్తుంది. ఈ loop లో current అంటే moving charge, అందుకే ఇది field తప్పక తయారు చేస్తుంది.

tts_sentences.text_en: Predict the shape. Take three seconds. What do the field lines around a current loop look like?
tts_sentences.text_hi: Shape predict करो। तीन seconds लो। एक current loop के आस-पास field lines कैसी दिखती हैं?
tts_sentences.text_te: Shape predict చెయ్యండి. మూడు seconds తీసుకోండి. ఒక current loop చుట్టూ field lines ఎలా కనిపిస్తాయి?

tts_sentences.text_en: Here they are. The lines leave one face, curl right around through space, and come back into the other face.
tts_sentences.text_hi: ये रहीं। Lines एक face से निकलती हैं, space में curl होकर, दूसरे face में वापस आ जाती हैं।
tts_sentences.text_te: ఇవిగో. Lines ఒక face నుండి బయటకు వెళ్ళి, space లో curl అయి, మళ్ళీ రెండో face లోకి తిరిగి వస్తాయి.

tts_sentences.text_en: Closed loops in three dimensions — not a flat circle around the wire. That shape is the clue to what comes next.
tts_sentences.text_hi: तीन dimensions में closed loops — wire के चारों तरफ एक flat circle नहीं। वो shape ही अगली बात का clue है।
tts_sentences.text_te: మూడు dimensions లో closed loops — wire చుట్టూ ఒక flat circle కాదు. ఆ shape అనేది తర్వాత వచ్చే విషయానికి clue.

misconception_watch.belief: Current only makes circular field lines around the wire, not a looped dipole pattern through the loop.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: Each bit of the ring does circle its wire, but adding all the bits together gives a field that threads through the loop and closes far away — the dipole pattern.

STATE_3.title: One face is North, one is South

scene_composition.text: The two faces of the loop are N and S poles

Field lines LEAVE → North face
Field lines ENTER → South face
The loop has TWO poles.

Could you ever get just
a single North pole on its own?
Predict, then watch.
No. The two faces always come together. There is no single magnetic pole.

tts_sentences.text_en: Look at the face where the lines leave. That side acts as a north pole.
tts_sentences.text_hi: जिस face से lines निकल रही हैं, उसे देखो। वो side north pole की तरह act करता है।
tts_sentences.text_te: Lines బయటకు వెళ్ళే face చూడండి. ఆ side north pole లాగా act చేస్తుంది.

tts_sentences.text_en: Look at the face where the lines come back in. That side acts as a south pole.
tts_sentences.text_hi: जिस face में lines वापस आ रही हैं, उसे देखो। वो side south pole की तरह act करता है।
tts_sentences.text_te: Lines తిరిగి వచ్చే face చూడండి. ఆ side south pole లాగా act చేస్తుంది.

tts_sentences.text_en: So a single loop already has two poles — north on one face, south on the other.
tts_sentences.text_hi: तो एक single loop में पहले से ही दो poles हैं — एक face पर north, दूसरे face पर south।
tts_sentences.text_te: అంటే ఒక single loop లో ఇప్పటికే రెండు poles ఉన్నాయి — ఒక face పై north, మరో face పై south.

tts_sentences.text_en: Could you keep just the north? No. The two faces are one object. A single magnetic pole does not exist.
tts_sentences.text_hi: क्या तुम सिर्फ north रख सकते हो? नहीं। दोनों faces एक ही object हैं। एक single magnetic pole exist नहीं करता।
tts_sentences.text_te: North ని మాత్రమే ఉంచగలవా? లేదు. రెండు faces ఒకే object. ఒక single magnetic pole exist అవదు.

misconception_watch.belief: A current loop has a single north pole, like a single point source.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: Every loop has BOTH faces at once — a north face and a south face. You can never isolate one; there are no magnetic monopoles.

STATE_4.title: This IS a bar magnet's field

scene_composition.text: Loop field = bar-magnet field

On the left: a bar magnet.
On the right: our current loop.
Compare the two field patterns.
Same or different?

Identical. Same closed loops,
same N and S, same shape.
The current loop IS a dipole.
Swap the loop for a bar magnet and nothing outside changes. They are the same kind of object.

tts_sentences.text_en: Now bring a bar magnet alongside the loop. There it is, on the left.
tts_sentences.text_hi: अब एक bar magnet को loop के पास लाते हैं। देखो, वो left side पर है।
tts_sentences.text_te: ఇప్పుడు ఒక bar magnet ని loop పక్కన తీసుకొద్దాం. అదిగో, left side లో ఉంది.

tts_sentences.text_en: Both are drawn the same way. Compare the two field patterns. Take three seconds — same or different?
tts_sentences.text_hi: दोनों को same तरीके से draw किया गया है। दोनों field patterns compare करो। तीन seconds लो — same है या different?
tts_sentences.text_te: రెండింటినీ same విధంగా draw చేశారు. రెండు field patterns compare చెయ్యండి. మూడు seconds తీసుకోండి — same గా ఉన్నాయా లేదా different?

tts_sentences.text_en: Look carefully. The loops of field line have the same shape, the same north and south.
tts_sentences.text_hi: ध्यान से देखो। field line के loops का shape same है, north and south भी same हैं।
tts_sentences.text_te: జాగ్రత్తగా చూడండి. field line loops అన్నింటికీ same shape ఉంది, same north and south కూడా.

tts_sentences.text_en: They are identical. Outside, a current loop's field is exactly a bar magnet's field.
tts_sentences.text_hi: वो identical हैं। बाहर, एक current loop का field बिल्कुल एक bar magnet के field जैसा ही है।
tts_sentences.text_te: అవి identical గా ఉన్నాయి. బయట, ఒక current loop యొక్క field సరిగ్గా ఒక bar magnet యొక్క field లాంటిదే.

tts_sentences.text_en: That is the big idea. A current loop IS a magnetic dipole. Swap one for the other and nothing outside changes.
tts_sentences.text_hi: यही है big idea। एक current loop ही एक magnetic dipole है। एक को दूसरे से swap करो — बाहर कुछ नहीं बदलेगा।
tts_sentences.text_te: ఇదే big idea. ఒక current loop అనేది magnetic dipole. ఒకదాన్ని మరొకదానితో swap చెయ్యి — బయట ఏమీ మారదు.

misconception_watch.belief: A current loop and a bar magnet are fundamentally different kinds of thing.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: Outside, their fields are identical. A bar magnet is just enormously many atomic current loops added up — the loop and the magnet are the same kind of object: a magnetic dipole.

STATE_5.title: The moment m = N I A

scene_composition.text: The magnetic moment m = N I A

m is a VECTOR.
Size: N · I · A  (A = π r²)
Direction: along the axis,
RHR — curl fingers with I,
thumb points along m.

Double the turns → double m.
Double the current → double m.
The yellow arrow is m.
m is the single number that sets how strong this dipole is — for a loop OR a bar magnet.

tts_sentences.text_en: How strong is this dipole? One quantity answers it: the magnetic moment m, equal to N times I times A.
tts_sentences.text_hi: यह dipole कितना strong है? एक quantity ही जवाब देती है: magnetic moment m, जो N times I times A के बराबर है।
tts_sentences.text_te: ఈ dipole ఎంత strong? ఒక quantity మాత్రమే జవాబు చెప్తుంది: magnetic moment m, అది N times I times A కి సమానం.
tts_sentences.math_show: m = N I A,\quad A = \pi r^2

tts_sentences.text_en: It is a vector, not just a number. The yellow arrow points along the loop axis.
tts_sentences.text_hi: यह सिर्फ एक number नहीं, यह एक vector है। Yellow arrow, loop axis के साथ-साथ point करता है।
tts_sentences.text_te: ఇది కేవలం ఒక number కాదు, ఇది ఒక vector. Yellow arrow, loop axis వెంట point చేస్తుంది.

tts_sentences.text_en: Its direction is the right-hand rule. Curl your fingers along the current; your thumb points along magnetic moment m.
tts_sentences.text_hi: इसकी direction right-hand rule से मिलती है। अपनी fingers को current के साथ curl करो; thumb magnetic moment m की direction में point करेगा।
tts_sentences.text_te: దీని direction అనేది right-hand rule వల్ల వస్తుంది. మీ fingers ని current వెంట curl చెయ్యండి; thumb magnetic moment m వెంట point చేస్తుంది.

tts_sentences.text_en: More turns, more current, or more area — all make magnetic moment m bigger, and the dipole stronger.
tts_sentences.text_hi: ज्यादा turns, ज्यादा current, या ज्यादा area — सब magnetic moment m को बड़ा बनाते हैं, और dipole को stronger।
tts_sentences.text_te: ఎక్కువ turns, ఎక్కువ current, లేదా ఎక్కువ area — అన్నీ magnetic moment m ని పెద్దగా చేస్తాయి, dipole ని stronger గా చేస్తాయి.

misconception_watch.belief: The magnetic moment is just a number that measures strength.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: m is a vector. Its size N·I·A sets the strength AND its direction (along the axis, by the right-hand rule) sets which way the dipole points — both matter.

STATE_6.title: Put it in an external field

scene_composition.text: In an external field, the dipole feels a torque

Blue arrows = external field B.
Opposite sides feel opposite
forces — a couple.
Net force zero, but it turns.

τ = m × B
The purple arrow is the torque,
same as for a bar magnet
in the same field.
A bar magnet in this field would feel the very same torque.

tts_sentences.text_en: Now switch on a uniform external field — the blue arrows, magnetic field B.
tts_sentences.text_hi: अब एक uniform external field switch on करते हैं — वो blue arrows, magnetic field B।
tts_sentences.text_te: ఇప్పుడు ఒక uniform external field switch on చేద్దాం — ఆ blue arrows, magnetic field B.

tts_sentences.text_en: Opposite sides of the loop feel opposite forces. Together they make a couple: no net push, but a turning effect.
tts_sentences.text_hi: Loop के opposite sides को opposite forces feel होती हैं। मिलकर वो एक couple बनाते हैं: कोई net push नहीं, पर एक turning effect जरूर है।
tts_sentences.text_te: Loop యొక్క opposite sides కి opposite forces అనిపిస్తాయి. కలిసి అవి ఒక couple తయారు చేస్తాయి: net push లేదు, కానీ ఒక turning effect ఉంటుంది.

tts_sentences.text_en: That turning effect is the torque, τ equals m cross B — the purple arrow.
tts_sentences.text_hi: वो turning effect ही torque है, τ equals m cross B — वो purple arrow।
tts_sentences.text_te: ఆ turning effect అనేది torque, τ equals m cross B — ఆ purple arrow.

tts_sentences.text_en: A bar magnet of the same moment in the same field would feel exactly this torque.
tts_sentences.text_hi: इसी field में इसी moment का bar magnet exactly यही torque feel करेगा।
tts_sentences.text_te: అదే field లో అదే moment గల bar magnet సరిగ్గా ఇదే torque అనుభవిస్తుంది.

scene_composition.text: The torque turns m to line up with B

The loop swings back and forth,
then settles with m along B —
exactly like a compass needle
settling to north.

No permanent magnet needed.
A loop of current responds to
the field all by itself.
This is exactly how the galvanometer coil deflects when current flows.

tts_sentences.text_en: Watch what the torque does. The loop swings, back and forth, and settles with magnetic moment m lined up along magnetic field B.
tts_sentences.text_hi: देखो torque क्या करता है। Loop आगे-पीछे swing करता है, और magnetic moment m को magnetic field B के साथ line up करके settle हो जाता है।
tts_sentences.text_te: Torque ఏం చేస్తోందో చూడండి. Loop ముందూ వెనకా swing అవుతుంది, మరియు magnetic moment m ని magnetic field B వెంట line up చేసి settle అవుతుంది.

tts_sentences.text_en: That is exactly a compass needle finding north. The needle is just a magnetic dipole, and so is this loop.
tts_sentences.text_hi: यह बिल्कुल वैसा ही है जैसे एक compass needle north ढूंढती है। वो needle सिर्फ एक magnetic dipole है, और यह loop भी।
tts_sentences.text_te: ఇది సరిగ్గా ఒక compass needle north వెతికినట్లే. ఆ needle కేవలం ఒక magnetic dipole, ఈ loop కూడా అంతే.

tts_sentences.text_en: And notice — no permanent magnet was needed. A plain loop of current responds to the field on its own.
tts_sentences.text_hi: और ध्यान दो — कोई permanent magnet जरूरी नहीं था। current का एक plain loop field पर खुद ही respond करता है।
tts_sentences.text_te: మరియు గమనించండి — ఏ permanent magnet అవసరం లేదు. current యొక్క ఒక plain loop field కి తనంత తానే respond చేస్తుంది.

misconception_watch.belief: You need a permanent magnet for something to respond to a magnetic field.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: A current loop is already a magnet while the current flows — it aligns and turns in a field just like a compass needle, with no permanent magnet anywhere.

scene_composition.text: Atoms are tiny current loops

An electron orbiting a nucleus
is nature's smallest current loop
— a tiny magnetic dipole,
with its own m = N I A.

Predict: where does a bar magnet's
magnetism actually come from?
From countless atomic current loops, all adding up. No monopoles anywhere — just loops.

tts_sentences.text_en: Here is why this matters so much. An electron orbiting in an atom is a tiny current loop.
tts_sentences.text_hi: यह इतना important क्यों है? क्योंकि atom में orbit करने वाला electron एक tiny current loop होता है।
tts_sentences.text_te: ఇది ఎందుకు ఇంత important? ఒక atom లో orbit చేసే electron ఒక tiny current loop.

tts_sentences.text_en: So every atom carries tiny magnetic dipoles. Predict — where does a bar magnet's magnetism come from?
tts_sentences.text_hi: तो हर atom में tiny magnetic dipoles होते हैं। Predict करो — एक bar magnet का magnetism आता कहाँ से है?
tts_sentences.text_te: కాబట్టి ప్రతి atom లో tiny magnetic dipoles ఉంటాయి. Predict చెయ్యండి — ఒక bar magnet యొక్క magnetism ఎక్కడ నుండి వస్తుంది?

tts_sentences.text_en: From all those atomic loops adding up. Magnetism is built from current loops — never from single magnetic poles.
tts_sentences.text_hi: उन सभी atomic loops के जुड़ने से। Magnetism current loops से बनता है — कभी single magnetic poles से नहीं।
tts_sentences.text_te: ఆ అన్ని atomic loops కలవడం వల్ల. Magnetism అనేది current loops నుండి నిర్మితమవుతుంది — ఎన్నడూ single magnetic poles నుండి కాదు.

misconception_watch.belief: Magnetism needs a permanent magnet, and magnetic monopoles (single poles) exist.
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: All magnetism comes from current loops — moving charges in atoms. Every source is a dipole; an isolated single magnetic pole has never been found.

scene_composition.text: Explore: change N, I, B and θ

N and I set m (the yellow arrow).
B and θ set the torque (purple).
Drag and watch them respond.

Challenge:
Double N — does m double?
Set θ = 0 — where does τ go?
m = N I A and τ = m B sin θ — the same rules a bar magnet obeys.

tts_sentences.text_en: Your turn. Drag the sliders for turns N, current I, magnetic field B and the angle.
tts_sentences.text_hi: अब तुम्हारी बारी। turns N, current I, magnetic field B और angle के sliders drag करो।
tts_sentences.text_te: ఇప్పుడు మీ వంతు. turns N, current I, magnetic field B మరియు angle కోసం sliders drag చెయ్యండి.

tts_sentences.text_en: More turns or more current grows the magnetic moment m. The field and angle set the torque.
tts_sentences.text_hi: ज्यादा turns या ज्यादा current, magnetic moment m को बढ़ाते हैं। Field और angle, torque को set करते हैं।
tts_sentences.text_te: ఎక్కువ turns లేదా ఎక్కువ current, magnetic moment m ని పెంచుతాయి. Field మరియు angle, torque ని set చేస్తాయి.
tts_sentences.math_show: m = N I A,\quad \tau = m B \sin\theta

tts_sentences.text_en: Try it. Double the turns and watch the magnetic moment m double. Set the angle to zero and watch the torque vanish.
tts_sentences.text_hi: Try करो। turns को double करो और देखो magnetic moment m double होता है। angle को zero पर set करो और देखो torque vanish हो जाता है।
tts_sentences.text_te: Try చెయ్యండి. Turns ని double చెయ్యండి మరియు magnetic moment m double అవడం చూడండి. Angle ని zero కి set చెయ్యండి మరియు torque vanish అవడం చూడండి.

STATE_1.label: A loop of current — dots flowing, no field yet
STATE_1.caption: A circular current loop. Orange dots show I flowing around the ring. No field shown yet.

STATE_2.label: The loop's own dipole field lines appear
STATE_2.caption: The loop sources a magnetic field: closed lines leave one face, curl around, and return to the other.

STATE_3.label: N and S faces labelled
STATE_3.caption: Lines leave the north face, enter the south face. Two poles — no single pole exists.

STATE_4.label: Side-by-side with a bar magnet — fields coincide (AHA)
STATE_4.caption: A bar magnet (left) beside the loop (right). Both field patterns are identical — the loop IS a dipole.

STATE_5.label: m vector along the axis — m = N I A
STATE_5.caption: The magnetic moment m = NIA, a vector along the loop axis by the right-hand rule. Observe-only.

STATE_6.label: External field on — couple + torque
STATE_6.caption: Uniform B switched on. Opposite sides feel opposite forces — a couple. τ = m × B appears.

STATE_7.label: Aligns like a compass — oscillates about θ = 0
STATE_7.caption: The torque swings the loop and settles m along B — exactly like a compass needle. No permanent magnet needed.

STATE_8.label: Origin of magnetism — loop + its field
STATE_8.caption: Electrons orbiting in atoms are tiny current loops. All magnetism is built from loops like this one.

STATE_9.label: Sandbox — N, I, B, θ sliders
STATE_9.caption: Drag N, I, B and θ. m = NIA scales the yellow arrow; τ = m B sinθ scales the purple arrow.

assessment.mastery_definition: A student has mastered this concept when they can state that a current loop produces a magnetic field whose EXTERNAL pattern is identical to a bar magnet's (the loop IS a magnetic dipole), that the loop has two poles — a north face where field lines leave and a south face where they enter, never a single pole — that the magnetic dipole moment m = N I A is a VECTOR along the loop axis (direction by the right-hand rule, magnitude growing with turns, current and area), that in an external field the dipole feels a torque τ = m × B that aligns m with B exactly as a compass needle aligns, and that atomic current loops (orbiting electrons) are the microscopic origin of all magnetism, so every magnetic source is a dipole and no magnetic monopole exists.

questions.stem: Far from a current-carrying loop, the magnetic field pattern looks like the field of:

distractor_misconceptions.B: Thinks a loop can produce a single (monopole) field
distractor_misconceptions.C: Sees only the local circular field of the wire, not the loop's dipole field
distractor_misconceptions.D: Confuses the magnetic dipole field with a radial electric field

questions.tested_idea: A current loop's external field is identical to a bar magnet's
questions.parallel_form_stem: Standing well away from a small coil carrying current, the magnetic field you measure matches that of which familiar object?

questions.stem: On a current loop, which face acts as the north pole?

options.A: The face the magnetic field lines leave
options.B: The face the magnetic field lines enter
options.C: Neither — a loop has only one pole

distractor_misconceptions.B: Reverses the convention for the north face
distractor_misconceptions.C: Thinks a loop is a magnetic monopole
distractor_misconceptions.D: Doesn't realise the two faces are opposite poles

questions.tested_idea: The face field lines leave is north; the loop has two poles
questions.parallel_form_stem: Field lines come OUT of one face of a current loop. What kind of pole is that face?

questions.stem: The magnetic dipole moment of a loop is m = N I A. Which statement is correct?

options.A: m is a vector pointing along the loop axis, with direction set by the right-hand rule
options.B: m is just a number measuring field strength, with no direction
options.C: m points along the current in the wire
options.D: m depends only on the current, not on the area or turns

distractor_misconceptions.B: Treats the moment as a scalar with no direction
distractor_misconceptions.C: Confuses the axial moment with the tangential current direction
distractor_misconceptions.D: Ignores the N and A factors in m = NIA

questions.tested_idea: m = NIA is a vector along the axis (RHR)
questions.parallel_form_stem: For a current loop, the quantity m = NIA — is it a scalar or a vector, and which way does it point?

questions.stem: You double the number of turns N in the loop (same current, same radius). The magnetic moment m:

distractor_misconceptions.B: Thinks N does not affect the moment
distractor_misconceptions.D: Confuses the linear N dependence with an area-like square

questions.tested_idea: m = NIA scales linearly with N
questions.parallel_form_stem: A coil's turns are doubled with current and size unchanged. By what factor does its magnetic moment change?

questions.stem: A current loop is placed in a uniform external magnetic field. What does it do, and why?

options.A: It feels a torque and turns to align m with B, like a compass needle
options.B: It is pushed bodily across the field with a large net force
options.C: Nothing, because only permanent magnets respond to fields

distractor_misconceptions.B: Thinks the couple gives a net translational force
distractor_misconceptions.C: Thinks only permanent magnets respond to a field
distractor_misconceptions.D: Confuses mechanical torque with a change in current

questions.tested_idea: In an external field the dipole feels τ = m × B and aligns
questions.parallel_form_stem: Switch on a uniform field around a current loop. How does the loop respond, and what is the reason?

questions.stem: Where does the magnetism of a permanent bar magnet ultimately come from?

options.A: Countless atomic current loops (orbiting/spinning electrons) adding up
options.B: A supply of isolated north and south magnetic monopoles inside it
options.C: Static electric charges sitting on its surface
options.D: A single giant current loop wrapped around the whole magnet

distractor_misconceptions.B: Believes magnetic monopoles exist inside the magnet
distractor_misconceptions.D: Misses that it is many atomic loops, not one macroscopic loop

questions.tested_idea: Atomic current loops are the origin of all magnetism; no monopoles
questions.parallel_form_stem: At the deepest level, what produces the field of an ordinary iron bar magnet?
